slides.title: Reading the Evidence Trail
slides.subtitle: Detecting Attacks on Devices: how logs and indicators of compromise reveal an attack, how to choose and evaluate a detection method, and how to spot password attacks line by line in authentication logs
slides.footerLeft: AP Cybersecurity · College Board CED (Effective Fall 2026)
slides.script: Welcome to Unit 4, Securing Devices, and Lesson 4.4, Detecting Attacks on Devices — a single comprehensive day. Every action a device takes leaves a trace: a login attempt, a file download, a configuration change. Today you learn to read that evidence trail. We move through four learning objectives — how to detect attacks, how to choose a detection control, how to evaluate that control's impact, and finally how to read an authentication log and name the password attack hiding in it. By the end you'll be able to look at a log excerpt and point to the indicator of compromise.

slides.heading: By the End of Today You Will...

items.text: Explain how computing systems log activity and identify host-based, file-based, and behavior-based indicators of compromise (LO 4.4.A)
Choose a detection control by weighing performance, cost, and the sensitivity of the device (LO 4.4.B)
Evaluate a detection method's impact — speed, attack phase, and false positives versus ease of bypass (LO 4.4.C)
Read authentication logs to identify online password attacks, password spraying, and credential stuffing (LO 4.4.D)

slides.script: Four objectives today, one for each learning objective in the topic. First, 4.4.A: how systems log activity and the three flavors of indicator of compromise — host-based, file-based, and behavior-based. Second, 4.4.B: how to pick a detection control by weighing performance, cost, and how sensitive the device is. Third, 4.4.C: how to evaluate the impact of that method — its speed, the attack phase it catches, and the trade-off between false positives and ease of bypass. Fourth, 4.4.D: the hands-on payoff, reading an authentication log to name an online password attack, password spraying, or credential stuffing. Keep these four in view; everything today maps back to one of them.

slides.prompt: An analyst opens a server's authentication log after a suspected break-in. The log records every login attempt — who tried, from what IP address, at what time, and whether it succeeded.
slides.subprompt: Write 3–5 sentences: what patterns in that log might make you suspect an attack? List at least three specific things you'd look for, and explain what each one would tell you.
slides.directions: Independent writing. I will cold-call 2–3 students.
slides.script: Two minutes, on your own, no notes. An analyst is staring at an authentication log after a suspected break-in — every login attempt with the user, the source IP, the time, and whether it worked. In three to five sentences, list at least three patterns that would make you suspicious, and say what each would tell you. Think about repetition, timing, location. Hold your answers — by the end of class every pattern you name will have a precise CED label and an EK number, from a burst of failed logins to a flood of users from one strange IP.

slides.heading: What You’ll Be Filling In
slides.subheading: Your Guided Notes have four sections that match today’s four objectives. Green = CB required. Blue = enrichment.

sections.title: Logs and Indicators of Compromise
sections.description: What computing systems log, what an IoC is, and the three IoC types — host-based, file-based, and behavior-based (EK 4.4.A.1–A.6).

sections.title: Choosing a Detection Control
sections.description: Weighing performance, cost (including EDR services), and device sensitivity to pick signature-based, anomaly-based, or hybrid detection (EK 4.4.B.1–B.3).

sections.title: Evaluating the Impact
sections.description: Speed and performance, the attack phase detection catches, and false positives versus ease of bypass (EK 4.4.C.1–C.3).

sections.title: Reading Logs for Password Attacks
sections.description: Spotting online password attacks, password spraying, and credential stuffing — and why offline attacks leave no trace (EK 4.4.D.1–D.5).

slides.note: Fill in your Guided Notes as we go. The Lesson 4.4 page on apcsexamprep.com has every answer if you fall behind.
slides.script: Four sections in your notes, one per objective. Section one is the foundation: what gets logged, what an indicator of compromise is, and the three IoC types. Section two is the decision: how to choose a detection control from performance, cost, and sensitivity. Section three is the evaluation: judging that method's impact on speed, attack phase, and the false-positive versus bypass trade-off. Section four is the hands-on finish: reading auth logs to name password attacks. Keep your pencil moving; I collect Guided Notes at the end of class.

slides.label: Logs and Indicators of Compromise
slides.kicker: Detecting Attacks on Devices · Learning Objective 4.4.A
slides.script: Section one. Learning objective 4.4.A: how to detect attacks against devices. It all starts with logs — the evidence trail — and the indicators of compromise we read out of them. Three IoC types to learn here, so stay with me.

slides.heading: Logs Are the Evidence Trail (EK 4.4.A.1–A.3)
bullets: Computing systems log system processes and settings, login attempts, file download attempts, and user actions (EK 4.4.A.1)
Those logs can be used to reconstruct the circumstances leading up to and during a cyber incident (EK 4.4.A.1)
An indicator of compromise (IoC) is evidence that an adversary has compromised a device or network (EK 4.4.A.2)
Authentication logs (auth logs) record every attempted login on a system; analyzing them can reveal attempted attacks (EK 4.4.A.3)
slides.script: Start with the foundation. Computing systems log four kinds of activity — system processes and settings, login attempts, file download attempts, and user actions, EK 4.4.A.1 — and those logs let an analyst reconstruct what happened before and during an incident. An indicator of compromise, or IoC, EK 4.4.A.2, is simply evidence that an adversary has compromised a device or network — it's the trail we follow. And one log matters more than any other today: the authentication log, EK 4.4.A.3, which records every attempted login, so analyzing it can reveal attacks. Write all three EKs.

slides.heading: Three Types of Indicators of Compromise
slides.subheading: EK 4.4.A.4–A.6 — where each IoC type is found and what it looks like.

items.title: Host-based IoCs
items.summary: Found by analyzing logs and configuration settings. Examples: unusual files created or modified, unexpected processes or services, unauthorized changes to configuration settings, and unauthorized software installation or update.

items.title: File-based IoCs
items.summary: Found by analyzing files on a device, usually executable files. Examples: a file whose hash matches known malware, a file name known to be created by a piece of malware, and a file path associated with malicious activity.

items.title: Behavior-based IoCs
items.summary: Found by analyzing authentication and access logs. Examples: multiple failed login attempts, unusual login times or locations, unauthorized attempts to access sensitive data, and attempts to elevate user privileges.

slides.script: Three types of IoC, and the exam wants you to know where each one is found. Host-based, EK 4.4.A.4, come from logs and configuration settings — unusual files, unexpected processes, unauthorized config changes, software installed without permission. File-based, EK 4.4.A.5, come from analyzing files on the device, usually executables — a hash that matches known malware, a malware-associated file name, a suspicious file path. Behavior-based, EK 4.4.A.6, come from authentication and access logs — multiple failed logins, unusual login times or locations, attempts to reach sensitive data, attempts to elevate privileges. The tell is the source: configuration versus files versus logs. Get all three into your notes with examples.

slides.heading: Key Vocabulary (LO 4.4.A)

terms.definition: Evidence that an adversary has compromised a device or network (EK 4.4.A.2).
A log that records every attempted login on a system; analyzing it can reveal attempted attacks (EK 4.4.A.3).
An indicator discovered by analyzing logs and configuration settings, such as unexpected processes or unauthorized configuration changes (EK 4.4.A.4).
An indicator discovered by analyzing files on a device, usually executables — for example, a file whose hash matches known malware (EK 4.4.A.5).
An indicator discovered by analyzing authentication and access logs, such as multiple failed logins or unusual login times (EK 4.4.A.6).

slides.script: Five terms for section one. An indicator of compromise is evidence an adversary has gotten in. An authentication log records every login attempt. Then the three IoC types, each tied to where it's found: host-based from logs and configuration, file-based from files on the device, behavior-based from authentication and access logs. Memorize each by name and EK, because the exam hands you an example and asks for the type.

slides.label: Choosing a Detection Control
slides.kicker: Detecting Attacks on Devices · Learning Objective 4.4.B
slides.script: Section two. Learning objective 4.4.B: determining the right control for detecting attacks. Three criteria drive the choice — performance, cost, and the sensitivity of the device. Let's weigh them.

slides.heading: Performance and Cost Drive the Choice
slides.subheading: EK 4.4.B.1 and B.2 — system resources and dollars both shape which detection method fits.

left.title: Performance (EK 4.4.B.1)
left.definition: Detection tools consume memory and processing power
examples: Detection tools use system memory and processing power and can impact device performance
Anomaly-based tools use MORE system resources than signature-based tools
Signature-based detection is the better option for devices with less powerful resources
Many embedded devices lack the resources to run any detection tools at all

right.title: Cost (EK 4.4.B.2)
right.definition: Licenses and services have to be paid for
examples: Buyers must consider the cost of enough licenses for every device monitored
Some organizations buy an endpoint detection and response (EDR) service from a third-party vendor
EDR services are expensive but provide a holistic, unified approach
EDR typically includes a centralized alert platform for monitoring possible attacks

slides.footer: AP TIP: Performance (EK 4.4.B.1) is about system resources — anomaly-based costs more, so signature-based fits weak devices. Cost (EK 4.4.B.2) is about money — EDR is the expensive, unified, third-party option with a central alert platform.
slides.script: Two of the three criteria, side by side. On the left, performance, EK 4.4.B.1: detection tools eat memory and processing power, and anomaly-based tools eat more than signature-based. So for a weak device, signature-based is the better fit — and many embedded devices can't run any detection tool at all. On the right, cost, EK 4.4.B.2: you have to buy a license for every device you monitor, and some organizations instead buy an endpoint detection and response service, EDR, from a third-party vendor. EDR is expensive but unified and holistic, with a centralized alert platform. Write both EKs and note the keyword: anomaly equals more resources, EDR equals expensive but centralized.

slides.heading: Sensitivity of the Device (EK 4.4.B.3)
bullets: Sensitivity or criticality of the device is the third criterion for choosing a detection method (EK 4.4.B.3)
Devices that store or process sensitive information, or provide critical services, are more likely to be targeted
Those high-value devices benefit from a hybrid-detection model to offer maximum protection, when possible
Pair this with performance: a sensitive but resource-rich server can run hybrid; a weak embedded sensor cannot
slides.script: The third criterion is sensitivity, EK 4.4.B.3. A device that stores or processes sensitive information, or provides a critical service, is more likely to be a target — and those high-value devices benefit from a hybrid-detection model for maximum protection, when possible. Notice the phrase 'when possible': you still have to respect performance. A powerful, sensitive server can run hybrid; a tiny embedded sensor can't, no matter how sensitive its data. The exam loves to test that tension between sensitivity and resources, so write both ideas down.

slides.heading: Stop and Think — Independent Writing
slides.subheading: Both questions map to LO 4.4.A and LO 4.4.B. Complete on your Guided Notes packet.
prompts: An analyst finds an executable whose hash matches known malware, sitting in a file path tied to malicious activity. Name the IoC type and cite the EK. Then name one host-based and one behavior-based indicator from the CED and cite their EKs.

A security team must pick detection software for (a) a fleet of low-power embedded sensors and (b) a hospital server holding sensitive patient records. Recommend a detection approach for each, and justify it using performance, cost, or sensitivity with EK numbers.
slides.directions: Independent. 6 minutes. Full sentences. I will collect Guided Notes at the end of class.
slides.script: Two questions, six minutes, independent, full sentences. Question one: a malware-hash executable in a suspicious path — name the IoC type with its EK, then give one host-based and one behavior-based example with EKs. That's EK 4.4.A.5 for file-based, and you choose from A.4 and A.6 for the others. Question two: pick detection for low-power sensors versus a sensitive hospital server, and justify each with performance, cost, or sensitivity. Expect signature-based for the sensors, EK 4.4.B.1, and a hybrid model for the server, EK 4.4.B.3. Cite as you go. Begin.

slides.label: Evaluating the Impact
slides.kicker: Detecting Attacks on Devices · Learning Objective 4.4.C
slides.script: Section three. Learning objective 4.4.C: evaluating the impact of a detection method. Three factors — speed and performance, the phase of the attack you catch, and the trade-off between false positives and ease of bypass.

slides.heading: Three Factors in Evaluating Impact
slides.subheading: EK 4.4.C.1–C.3 — how speed, attack phase, and the false-positive trade-off shape the decision.

items.title: Speed and Performance
items.summary: Signature-based detection is faster than anomaly-based in general, and that effect is compounded on devices, which often lack the processing power to run anomaly-based tools effectively. Resource-intensive tools can degrade device performance.

items.title: Phase of the Attack
items.summary: To act on a device, adversaries must first bypass physical- or network-layer protective, deterrent, and detective controls. Detecting and stopping an attack at the device level can prevent access to sensitive data or disruption of critical services.

items.title: False Positives vs. Bypass
items.summary: Most device-level detection is signature-based, which has a low rate of false positives — but signature-based detection is also easier for adversaries to bypass. The two facts are separate trade-offs.

slides.script: Three factors for judging impact. EK 4.4.C.1, speed and performance: signature-based is faster than anomaly-based in general, and that gap grows on devices that can't run anomaly-based tools well — heavy tools degrade performance. EK 4.4.C.2, phase of the attack: by the time an adversary acts on a device, they've already bypassed physical- and network-layer controls, so catching the attack at the device level can be the last line that protects sensitive data or critical services. EK 4.4.C.3, the false-positive trade-off: most device-level detection is signature-based, which gives few false positives, but it's also easier to bypass. Keep those two facts separate. Write all three EKs.

slides.heading: Misconception: Fewer False Positives Means Harder to Bypass
slides.misconception: Signature-based detection has a low rate of false positives, so it must also be the hardest detection method for an attacker to get past.
slides.correction: EK 4.4.C.3 — those are two separate trade-offs. Signature-based detection does have a low false-positive rate, but it is also EASIER for adversaries to bypass. A low false-positive rate says nothing about how resistant the method is to evasion.
slides.script: A trap worth flagging. Students see 'low false positives' and assume that also means 'hard to bypass.' Not so. EK 4.4.C.3 keeps these as two separate trade-offs: signature-based detection is accurate, with few false alarms, but precisely because it matches known signatures it's easier for an adversary to slip past with something new. Low false positives is a reliability claim; ease of bypass is a security claim. Don't fuse them — the exam writes distractors that do exactly that.

slides.label: Reading Logs for Password Attacks
slides.kicker: Detecting Attacks on Devices · Learning Objective 4.4.D
slides.script: Section four, the hands-on finish. Learning objective 4.4.D: applying detection to identify password attacks in log files. We'll learn the signatures of each attack, then read an actual auth-log excerpt together.

slides.heading: The Signatures of Each Password Attack (EK 4.4.D.1–D.5)
bullets: Online password attack: a single user account attempting many wrong passwords in a short span (EK 4.4.D.1)
Compromised account: an authorized user logging in from an unexpected location, IP, or time may mean their password is stolen (EK 4.4.D.2)
Password spraying: many different users trying to log in within seconds of each other from one IP or unusual IPs (EK 4.4.D.3)
Credential stuffing: a series of default user:password combinations tried in quick succession, often from the same IP (EK 4.4.D.4)
Offline password attacks can’t be detected — they happen on the adversary’s own computer (EK 4.4.D.5)
slides.script: Here are the signatures you'll match in the log. EK 4.4.D.1, online password attack: one user account, many wrong passwords, fast. EK 4.4.D.2, a compromised account: a real user logging in from an unexpected place, IP, or time — a sign the password was stolen. EK 4.4.D.3, password spraying: many different users, one or a few odd IPs, all within seconds. EK 4.4.D.4, credential stuffing: default user-and-password pairs tried in quick succession, usually from one IP. And EK 4.4.D.5, the one that catches students: offline attacks can't be detected at all, because they run on the adversary's own machine and never touch your logs. Write all five.

slides.heading: Worked Example: Reading an Authentication Log for IoCs
slides.context: An analyst reviews this authentication-log excerpt from one server. Each row is one login attempt. Read it line by line and name the indicator — then the attack.
rows: Normal: expected user, internal IP, business hours
One user, 9 wrong passwords in seconds → online password attack (EK 4.4.D.1)
Authorized user, unusual time + new IP → possible compromised password (EK 4.4.D.2)
Many users, one unusual IP, within seconds → password spraying (EK 4.4.D.3)
Default user:password pairs in quick succession, one IP → credential stuffing (EK 4.4.D.4)
slides.note: Note what is NOT here: an offline password attack leaves no rows at all — it runs on the adversary's computer and never reaches this log (EK 4.4.D.5).
slides.script: Let's read it together, top to bottom. Row one is normal: an expected user, an internal IP, business hours — no indicator. Row two: user m.lee, nine failed passwords in nine seconds from one external IP — that's one user, many wrong passwords, an online password attack, EK 4.4.D.1. Row three: m.lee again, but this time a success at 2:47 in the morning from a brand-new IP — an authorized account logging in at an unusual time and place, a possible compromised password, EK 4.4.D.2. Row four: a flood of different usernames, all from one unusual IP, within seconds — that's password spraying, EK 4.4.D.3. Row five: default pairs like admin-admin and root-root tried in quick succession from one IP — credential stuffing, EK 4.4.D.4. And the thing that's missing? An offline attack — it would leave no rows here at all, EK 4.4.D.5. That's the whole objective in one table.

slides.heading: Stop and Think — Independent Writing
slides.subheading: Both questions map to LO 4.4.C and LO 4.4.D. Complete on your Guided Notes packet.
prompts: An auth log shows hundreds of different usernames each attempting one login within a five-second window, all from a single unfamiliar IP address. Name the attack, cite the EK, and explain how its log signature differs from a single user trying many wrong passwords.

An adversary steals a password-hash database and cracks it entirely on their own computer. Explain, using the CED, why this attack produces no entries in the target's authentication log — and contrast that with why an online attack does.
slides.directions: Independent. 6 minutes. Full sentences. I will collect Guided Notes at the end of class.
slides.script: Last two questions, six minutes, independent, full sentences. Question one: hundreds of different users, one login each, five-second window, one unfamiliar IP — name the attack and cite the EK, then contrast its signature with a single user trying many passwords. That's password spraying, EK 4.4.D.3, versus an online password attack, EK 4.4.D.1. Question two: an adversary cracks a stolen hash database on their own machine — explain why your logs stay empty, and contrast that with an online attack that does leave a trail. Lean on EK 4.4.D.5. Cite as you write. Go.

slides.heading: Detecting Attacks on Devices, in One Slide
points: Logs record processes, logins, downloads, and user actions, and let analysts reconstruct an incident; an IoC is evidence of compromise (EK 4.4.A.1–A.2)
IoCs come in three types by source: host-based (logs/config), file-based (files), and behavior-based (auth/access logs) (EK 4.4.A.4–A.6)
Choose a detection control by performance, cost, and sensitivity — signature-based for weak devices, EDR for unified monitoring, hybrid for critical ones (EK 4.4.B.1–B.3)
Evaluate impact by speed, attack phase, and false positives vs. bypass — signature-based is fast and accurate but easier to evade (EK 4.4.C.1–C.3)
Read auth logs to name password attacks: online (one user, many tries), spraying (many users, one IP), stuffing (default pairs) — offline attacks leave no trace (EK 4.4.D.1–D.5)
slides.next: Carry these detection skills into the Lesson 4.4 quiz — read every scenario for the indicator, then name the type and the attack with its EK number.
slides.script: Pull it all together. Logs are the evidence trail, and an IoC is evidence of compromise. IoCs come in three types by source — host-based, file-based, behavior-based. You choose a detection control by weighing performance, cost, and sensitivity: signature-based for weak devices, EDR when you want unified monitoring, hybrid for the most critical machines. You evaluate that method by speed, attack phase, and the false-positive-versus-bypass trade-off — signature-based is fast and accurate but easier to evade. And you read authentication logs to name the password attack: one user with many tries is online, many users from one IP is spraying, default pairs are stuffing, and an offline attack leaves nothing behind. That's the whole topic — now take it to the quiz.

guide.title: Detecting Attacks on Devices — Teacher Guide
guide.subtitle: Unit 4: Securing Devices   •   LO 4.4.A–4.4.D
overview: Topic 4.4 is the detection topic of Unit 4: students learn how computing systems log activity, what an indicator of compromise (IoC) is, and how to read authentication, user-activity, and access logs to spot host-based, file-based, and behavior-based IoCs. It then turns to the practical question of which detection method to use, weighing performance, cost, and device sensitivity, and finishes by applying those skills to recognize password attacks in log files.

The recurring idea is that logs are the evidence trail of an attack: most device-level detection is signature-based (fast, low false positives, but easier to bypass), while anomaly-based detection catches more but costs more system resources — and offline password attacks cannot be detected at all because they happen on the adversary's own computer.
los: LO 4.4.A — Explain how to detect attacks against devices.
LO 4.4.B — Determine controls for detecting attacks against a device.
LO 4.4.C — Evaluate the impact of a device detection method.
LO 4.4.D — Apply detection techniques to identify indicators of password attacks by analyzing log files.
eks: EK 4.4.A.1 — System processes and settings, login attempts, file download attempts, and user actions are logged by computing systems. These logs can be used to reconstruct circumstances leading up to and during a cyber incident.
EK 4.4.A.2 — An indicator of compromise (IoC) is evidence that an adversary has compromised a device or network.
EK 4.4.A.3 — Authentication logs (or auth logs) record every attempted login on a system. Analysis of authentication logs can reveal attempted attacks.

EK 4.4.A.4 — Host-based IoCs are discovered when analyzing logs and configuration settings. Indicators found in authentication logs, user activity logs, and system configuration files include: unusual files being created or modified; unexpected processes or services; unauthorized changes to system configuration settings; unauthorized software installation or update.

EK 4.4.A.5 — File-based IoCs are discovered when analyzing files on a device. Indicators are usually found in executable files and can include: files whose hash matches known malware; file names known to be created by a certain piece of malware; file paths associated with malicious activity.

EK 4.4.A.6 — Behavior-based IoCs are discovered when analyzing logs. Indicators found in authentication logs and access logs can include: multiple failed login attempts; unusual login times or locations; unauthorized attempts to access sensitive data; attempts to elevate user privileges on a system.

EK 4.4.B.1 — Performance is a criterion for determining a detection method. Detection tools use system memory and processing power and can impact device performance. Anomaly-based detection tools use more system resources than signature-based tools; signature-based detection is a better option for devices with less powerful system resources. Many embedded devices do not have enough system resources to run any detection tools on the device.

EK 4.4.B.2 — Cost is a criterion for determining a detection method. Organizations that purchase detection software must consider the cost of enough licenses for the number of devices monitored. Some organizations purchase an endpoint detection and response (EDR) service from a third-party vendor; these services are expensive but provide a holistic, unified approach and typically include a centralized alert platform for monitoring possible attacks.

EK 4.4.B.3 — Sensitivity or criticality of the device is a criterion for determining a detection method. Devices that store or process sensitive information or provide critical services are more likely to be targeted and benefit from a hybrid-detection model to offer maximum protection, when possible.

EK 4.4.C.1 — Speed and performance are factors in evaluating the impact of a detection method. Signature-based detection is faster than anomaly-based detection in general, and that effect is compounded on devices, which often lack the processing power to effectively run anomaly-based detection tools. Implementing resource-intensive detection tools on devices can degrade device performance.

EK 4.4.C.2 — Phase of the attack is a factor in evaluating impact. To carry out actions on a device, adversaries must first bypass a combination of physical- or network-layer protective, deterrent, and detective security controls. Detecting and stopping an attack at the device level can prevent adversaries from accessing sensitive data or disrupting critical services.

EK 4.4.C.3 — False positives versus ease of bypassing detection is a factor in evaluating impact. Most device-level detection tools are signature-based, and signature-based detection has a low rate of false positives. However, signature-based detection is easier for adversaries to bypass.

EK 4.4.D.1 — Online password attacks can be detected in authentication logs. A single user attempting many wrong passwords is an indicator of an online password attack. If a user:password hash database has been compromised, all the passwords should be considered insecure and all users forced to reset their passwords.

EK 4.4.D.2 — If an authorized user is logging in from a different location or IP address than expected, or at a different time than normal, this can be an indicator that the user's password has been compromised.
EK 4.4.D.3 — An indicator of password spraying is many users trying to log in within seconds of each other from one IP address or from unusual IP addresses.
EK 4.4.D.4 — An indicator of credential stuffing is a series of default user:password combinations being attempted on a device in quick succession, often from the same IP address.
EK 4.4.D.5 — Offline password attacks can't be detected, because the attack takes place on the adversary's computer.
materials: Projector for sample log excerpts and IoC examples
Sample authentication-log handout with failed-login and unusual-IP entries to annotate
prereqs: Topic 4.1–4.3 understanding of devices, device threats, and protective/detective controls
Familiarity with signature-based vs. anomaly-based detection and the idea of IP addresses and login events

pacing.focus: Logs, IoCs, and choosing a detection method (LO 4.4.A–B)

segments.segment: Bell ringer — what a log could reveal about an attack
Choosing a detection method: performance, cost, sensitivity (4.4.B)

pacing.focus: Evaluating impact and reading logs for IoCs (LO 4.4.C–D)

segments.segment: Evaluating impact: speed, attack phase, false positives vs. negatives (4.4.C)
Reading auth logs for password attacks — worked examples (4.4.D)

pacing.label: Flex / Assessment block — no new deck (uses the Lesson Quiz)
pacing.focus: Not a numbered prep day. Run it as a block-period extension or as the next lesson’s warm-up (doubles as spaced retrieval). The quiz already ships as its own document, so no new deck or notes are needed.

misconceptions.heading: Any IoC means an attack succeeded
misconceptions.myth: Finding an indicator of compromise proves the adversary got in and stole data.
misconceptions.reality: EK 4.4.A.2 — an IoC is evidence that an adversary has compromised a device or network; it is the evidence trail analysts investigate, and indicators such as failed logins (EK 4.4.A.6) can also point to attempted attacks revealed in the logs (EK 4.4.A.3).

misconceptions.heading: Anomaly-based detection is always the better choice
misconceptions.myth: You should always run anomaly-based detection on every device because it catches more.
misconceptions.reality: EK 4.4.B.1 — anomaly-based tools use more system resources than signature-based tools, so signature-based detection is a better option for devices with less powerful resources, and many embedded devices cannot run any detection tools at all.

misconceptions.heading: Signature-based detection is the safest because it has few false positives
misconceptions.myth: Low false positives means signature-based detection is the hardest for attackers to get past.
misconceptions.reality: EK 4.4.C.3 — signature-based detection does have a low rate of false positives, but it is also easier for adversaries to bypass; the two facts are separate trade-offs.

misconceptions.heading: All password attacks can be caught in the logs
misconceptions.myth: If you read the authentication logs carefully enough, you can detect any password attack.
misconceptions.reality: EK 4.4.D.5 — offline password attacks can't be detected, because the attack takes place on the adversary's computer; only online attacks such as spraying and credential stuffing leave traces in the logs.

support: Give students a three-column IoC chart (host-based, file-based, behavior-based) pre-filled with one CED example each, then have them sort additional indicators into the right column.
Walk through a short annotated auth-log sample, labeling each suspicious line as a failed-login burst, an unusual IP, or a normal entry before asking students to do their own.
Provide a sentence frame for method selection: 'For a device that is ___, I would choose ___ detection because ___ (performance / cost / sensitivity).'
extension: Have students compare two log excerpts — one showing many wrong passwords from one user and one showing many users from one IP — and name each attack (online password attack vs. password spraying) using EK 4.4.D.1 and D.3.
Ask students to justify a hybrid-detection model for a device that stores sensitive data using EK 4.4.B.3, then explain why the same model is impractical for an embedded sensor (EK 4.4.B.1).
Challenge students to explain why detecting an attack at the device level matters in terms of attack phase (EK 4.4.C.2) and what the adversary had to bypass to get there.

discussion: Why are logs described as the evidence used to reconstruct what happened before and during an incident?
When would you accept the higher resource cost of anomaly-based detection, and when would signature-based be the smarter call?
Why can online password attacks be spotted in the logs while offline attacks cannot?
guide.answerKeyNote: Each question's full rationale is on the KEY copy of the quiz (print it with the answer-key option). The short answer key below is pulled straight from the quiz on this page, so it always matches the questions students see.

quiz.title: Lesson 4.4 — Detecting Attacks on Devices — Quiz
quiz.instructions: Choose the single best answer. 12 questions, 25 minutes, no notes.

questions.stem: According to the CED, what is an indicator of compromise (IoC)?
options: Evidence that an adversary has compromised a device or network
A signed agreement that a vendor will repair a device after a breach
A setting that automatically encrypts a device once an attack is detected
A log entry that records every legitimate login an authorized user makes
questions.rationale: EK 4.4.A.2 — an indicator of compromise (IoC) is evidence that an adversary has compromised a device or network. The other options describe a vendor agreement, an automatic encryption setting, and a record of legitimate activity, none of which match the CED definition.

questions.stem: According to the CED, what do computing-system logs record, and why are they useful after a cyber incident?
options: Only the physical temperature of the device, used to predict hardware failures before they happen
System processes and settings, login attempts, file download attempts, and user actions, used to reconstruct the circumstances around an incident
Only outbound network packets, used to bill the organization for its monthly bandwidth usage
Only the names of installed applications, used to confirm software licenses are paid for
questions.rationale: EK 4.4.A.1 — system processes and settings, login attempts, file download attempts, and user actions are logged by computing systems, and these logs can be used to reconstruct the circumstances leading up to and during a cyber incident. The other options narrow logging to temperature, billing, or licensing, which the CED does not state.

questions.stem: An analyst finds that an executable file on a device has a hash that matches known malware and sits in a file path associated with malicious activity. According to the CED, which category of indicator of compromise is this?
options: A behavior-based IoC, discovered by analyzing patterns of login and access activity in logs
A host-based IoC, discovered by reviewing system configuration settings for unauthorized changes
A file-based IoC, discovered by analyzing files on a device, usually executable files
A network-based IoC, discovered by inspecting packet headers crossing the firewall
questions.rationale: EK 4.4.A.5 — file-based IoCs are discovered when analyzing files on a device, usually executable files, and can include files whose hash matches known malware and file paths associated with malicious activity. Behavior-based and host-based IoCs come from logs and configuration analysis, and the CED does not define a 'network-based IoC' in this topic.

questions.stem: Which of the following does the CED list as behavior-based IoCs found in authentication and access logs? I. Multiple failed login attempts. II. Unusual login times or locations. III. Attempts to elevate user privileges on a system. Which are included?
options: I and II only (failed logins and unusual times or locations, but not privilege elevation)
II only (only unusual login times or locations count as behavior-based)
I and III only (failed logins and privilege elevation, but not unusual times or locations)
I, II, and III (failed logins, unusual times or locations, and privilege elevation)
questions.rationale: EK 4.4.A.6 — behavior-based IoCs found in authentication and access logs include multiple failed login attempts, unusual login times or locations, unauthorized attempts to access sensitive data, and attempts to elevate user privileges on a system. All three listed indicators are included, so the answer is I, II, and III.

questions.stem: A security team is choosing detection software for a fleet of small embedded sensors that have very limited memory and processing power. According to the CED, which detection approach is the better fit, and why?
options: Anomaly-based detection, since it uses fewer system resources than signature-based detection on small devices
Signature-based detection, since it uses fewer system resources and is a better option for devices with less powerful resources
A full hybrid model on every sensor, since embedded devices always have spare resources for both methods
No detection method matters here, since detection tools have no effect on a device's performance
questions.rationale: EK 4.4.B.1 — anomaly-based tools use more system resources than signature-based tools, so signature-based detection is a better option for devices with less powerful resources, and many embedded devices cannot run any detection tools at all. The other options reverse the resource comparison, over-provision embedded devices, or deny that tools affect performance.

questions.stem: An organization does not want to manage detection in-house and instead buys a service from a third-party vendor that gives a holistic, unified approach with a centralized alert platform for monitoring possible attacks across its devices. According to the CED, what is this service?
options: A signature database update, which only refreshes malware hashes on each device weekly
A stateless firewall service, which filters traffic using packet-header information at the perimeter
A backup-and-recovery service, which restores devices to a clean state after an incident
An endpoint detection and response (EDR) service, which is expensive but provides unified, centralized threat detection
questions.rationale: EK 4.4.B.2 — some organizations purchase an endpoint detection and response (EDR) service from a third-party vendor; these services are expensive but provide a holistic, unified approach and typically include a centralized alert platform for monitoring possible attacks on devices. The other options describe updates, a firewall, and backups, not EDR.

questions.stem: A hospital server stores highly sensitive patient data and provides a critical service, making it a likely target for adversaries. According to the CED, what detection approach offers maximum protection for this device when possible?
options: A hybrid-detection model, since sensitive or critical devices benefit from combining detection methods
Removing detection entirely, since detection tools would slow down a critical service too much
Signature-only detection, since critical devices should minimize false positives above all else
Anomaly-only detection, since sensitive devices never have enough resources for signatures
questions.rationale: EK 4.4.B.3 — devices that store or process sensitive information or provide critical services are more likely to be targeted and benefit from a hybrid-detection model to offer maximum protection, when possible. The other options strip detection away or force a single method the CED does not prescribe for sensitive devices.

questions.stem: When evaluating the impact of a detection method on a device, an analyst compares signature-based and anomaly-based detection for speed. According to the CED, what is true about their speed on devices?
options: Anomaly-based detection is faster, and the speed gap grows on devices with limited processing power
Both methods run at identical speed, since detection speed does not depend on the device's resources
Signature-based detection is faster in general, and that effect is compounded on devices that lack processing power for anomaly-based tools
Speed is irrelevant, since resource-intensive detection tools never degrade a device's performance
questions.rationale: EK 4.4.C.1 — signature-based detection is faster than anomaly-based detection in general, and that effect is compounded on devices, which often lack the processing power to run anomaly-based tools effectively; resource-intensive tools can degrade device performance. The other options reverse the speed claim or deny any performance impact.

questions.stem: An analyst notes that the device-level detection in place has a very low rate of false positives but worries it is comparatively easy for a skilled adversary to evade. According to the CED, which detection type fits both observations?
options: Anomaly-based detection, which has a high false-positive rate and is very hard to bypass
Signature-based detection, which has a low false-positive rate but is easier for adversaries to bypass
Behavior-based detection, which eliminates false positives and cannot be bypassed at all
Hybrid detection, which removes the trade-off by making bypass impossible on every device
questions.rationale: EK 4.4.C.3 — most device-level detection tools are signature-based, signature-based detection has a low rate of false positives, but it is easier for adversaries to bypass. The other options invert the false-positive trait or claim that bypass becomes impossible, which the CED does not state.

questions.stem: An authentication log shows a single user account submitting many wrong passwords in a short span of time on one system. According to the CED, what does this pattern indicate?
options: Credential stuffing, in which default user:password combinations are tried across many devices
An offline password attack, which leaves this exact trail in the authentication log
Password spraying, in which many different users log in within seconds of each other
An online password attack, since one user attempting many wrong passwords is its indicator
questions.rationale: EK 4.4.D.1 — online password attacks can be detected in authentication logs, and a single user attempting many wrong passwords is an indicator of an online password attack. Credential stuffing and password spraying have different log signatures, and offline attacks leave no trail in the logs at all.

questions.stem: An analyst reviews two log excerpts. Excerpt 1: many different users each attempt a login within seconds of one another, all from one unusual IP address. Excerpt 2: a series of default user:password combinations is tried in quick succession from a single IP address. According to the CED, which attacks do these excerpts indicate?
options: Excerpt 1 is password spraying; Excerpt 2 is credential stuffing
Excerpt 1 is credential stuffing; Excerpt 2 is password spraying
Both excerpts indicate an offline password attack that happened on the adversary's computer
Both excerpts indicate a single user attempting many wrong passwords on one account
questions.rationale: EK 4.4.D.3 — an indicator of password spraying is many users trying to log in within seconds of each other from one or unusual IP addresses (Excerpt 1). EK 4.4.D.4 — an indicator of credential stuffing is a series of default user:password combinations attempted in quick succession, often from the same IP address (Excerpt 2). The other options swap the two or misclassify them as offline or single-user attacks.

questions.stem: An adversary obtains a copy of a password hash database and runs a password-cracking tool entirely on their own computer to recover the plaintext passwords. According to the CED, why can this attack not be detected in the target's authentication logs?
options: The logs only record file downloads, so any login-related attack is invisible to analysts
Detection tools always ignore privileged accounts, so this activity is never written to a log
Offline password attacks can't be detected, since the attack takes place on the adversary's computer
Signature-based detection deletes the relevant log entries before an analyst can review them
questions.rationale: EK 4.4.D.5 — offline password attacks can't be detected, because the attack takes place on the adversary's computer, so no login attempts reach the target's authentication logs. The other options invent log limitations, blind spots, or deletion behavior that the CED does not describe.
